description: European human rights and procedural framework for law enforcement. Sources: European Convention on Human Rights (ECHR), Charter of Fundamental Rights of the EU (CFR), UN Convention Against Torture (UNCAT), key ECtHR Grand Chamber precedents.

rules.title: Right to Life
rules.source: ECHR Article 2; McCann and Others v United Kingdom (1995), App. no. 18984/91
rules.summary: Use of lethal force by state agents must be no more than absolutely necessary in defence of any person from unlawful violence, to effect lawful arrest, or to quell riot. Planning and control of operations must minimise risk to life.
rules.subject: officer

rules.title: Prohibition of Torture
rules.source: ECHR Article 3; Selmouni v France (1999), App. no. 25803/94 (Grand Chamber)
rules.summary: Torture is absolutely prohibited — no derogation permitted even in war or public emergency. The threshold for 'torture' (as opposed to inhuman treatment) is severity of suffering inflicted intentionally for purpose of obtaining information or confession.
rules.subject: officer

rules.title: Inhuman or Degrading Treatment
rules.source: ECHR Article 3; Bouyid v Belgium (2015), App. no. 23380/09 (Grand Chamber)
rules.summary: Even a single slap by police to a person under their control violates Article 3 — any recourse to physical force against a person deprived of liberty which has not been made strictly necessary by their own conduct diminishes human dignity. Threat of torture also constitutes inhuman treatment.
rules.subject: officer

rules.title: Conditions of Detention
rules.source: ECHR Article 3; Kudła v Poland (2000), App. no. 30210/96
rules.summary: Detention conditions must respect human dignity. Overcrowding, lack of medical care, denial of food/water/sanitation can amount to degrading treatment regardless of officer intent.
rules.subject: officer

rules.title: Procedural Duty to Investigate Ill-Treatment
rules.source: ECHR Article 3 (procedural limb); Assenov v Bulgaria (1998), App. no. 24760/94
rules.summary: When credible allegation of ill-treatment by police is made, state must conduct effective, independent, prompt investigation. Failure to investigate is a separate Article 3 violation.
rules.subject: officer

rules.title: Right to Liberty — Lawful Arrest
rules.source: ECHR Article 5(1)(c); Fox, Campbell and Hartley v UK (1990), App. no. 12244/86
rules.summary: Arrest is lawful only on reasonable suspicion of having committed an offence. 'Reasonable suspicion' requires facts or information satisfying an objective observer.
rules.subject: officer

rules.title: Right to Be Informed of Arrest Reasons
rules.source: ECHR Article 5(2); Conka v Belgium (2002), App. no. 51564/99
rules.summary: Everyone arrested must be informed promptly, in a language they understand, of the reasons for arrest and any charge. Information must be sufficient to challenge lawfulness.
rules.subject: officer

rules.title: Prompt Production Before a Judge
rules.source: ECHR Article 5(3); Brogan v UK (1988), App. no. 11209/84
rules.summary: Arrested person must be brought promptly before a judge. 'Prompt' generally means within 48-96 hours; longer requires exceptional justification.
rules.subject: officer

rules.title: Right to a Fair Trial — Pre-Trial Phase
rules.source: ECHR Article 6(1); Salduz v Turkey (2008), App. no. 36391/02 (Grand Chamber)
rules.summary: Fair trial protections extend to pre-trial police interrogation. Statements obtained in violation of Article 6 cannot ground a conviction.
rules.subject: officer

rules.title: Right to Silence and Against Self-Incrimination
rules.source: ECHR Article 6(1); Funke v France (1993); Saunders v UK (1996)
rules.summary: Suspects have the right to remain silent and not contribute to incriminating themselves. Adverse inference from silence is restricted; coercion to speak violates Article 6.
rules.subject: officer

rules.title: Right of Access to a Lawyer
rules.source: ECHR Article 6(3)(c); Salduz v Turkey (2008), App. no. 36391/02 (Grand Chamber); EU Directive 2013/48/EU
rules.summary: Access to a lawyer must be granted from the first police interrogation. Statements made without lawyer present, absent compelling reasons, are presumptively inadmissible.
rules.subject: officer

rules.title: Right to an Interpreter
rules.source: ECHR Article 6(3)(e); EU Directive 2010/64/EU on the right to interpretation
rules.summary: A suspect who does not understand the language used must be provided with free interpretation during police interrogation and proceedings. Translation of essential documents is also required.
rules.subject: officer

rules.title: Right to Respect for Private Life
rules.source: ECHR Article 8; S. and Marper v UK (2008), App. no. 30562/04 (Grand Chamber)
rules.summary: Police interference with private life (searches, surveillance, retention of biometric data) must be lawful, pursue a legitimate aim, and be necessary and proportionate.
rules.subject: officer

rules.title: Bodycam Recording — Data Protection
rules.source: ECHR Article 8; GDPR Article 6 and Law Enforcement Directive (EU) 2016/680
rules.summary: Body-worn camera recordings constitute personal data processing. Must have legal basis, clear retention policy, subject access rights, and proportionality in deployment. Continuous recording without notice may violate Article 8.
rules.subject: officer

rules.title: Freedom of Expression — Criticism of Police
rules.source: ECHR Article 10; Janowski v Poland (1999), App. no. 25716/94
rules.summary: Verbal criticism, insults, or protest against police — even when offensive — is generally protected speech. Arrest as retaliation for protected expression violates Article 10.
rules.subject: officer

rules.title: Freedom of Peaceful Assembly
rules.source: ECHR Article 11; Kudrevičius v Lithuania (2015), App. no. 37553/05 (Grand Chamber)
rules.summary: Peaceful protesters may not be dispersed or arrested merely for participating in an unauthorised assembly. State must show tolerance toward peaceful gatherings; force is last resort.
rules.subject: officer

rules.title: Prohibition of Discrimination
rules.source: ECHR Article 14; Timishev v Russia (2005), App. no. 55762/00
rules.summary: Police treatment must not discriminate on grounds of race, ethnicity, religion, gender. Ethnic profiling in stops and searches without objective justification violates Article 14 read with Article 8.
rules.subject: officer

rules.title: Non-Refoulement
rules.source: ECHR Article 3; Saadi v Italy (2008), App. no. 37201/06 (Grand Chamber); Hirsi Jamaa v Italy (2012)
rules.summary: States may not expel, deport, or return any person to a country where there are substantial grounds to believe they face a real risk of torture or inhuman treatment. Applies absolutely, including at maritime borders.
rules.subject: officer

rules.title: Human Dignity — Inviolable
rules.source: Charter of Fundamental Rights of the EU, Article 1
rules.summary: Human dignity is inviolable and must be respected and protected. Treats this as foundational principle informing interpretation of all other rights — especially relevant when subject is restrained, vulnerable, or in custody.
rules.subject: officer

rules.title: Prohibition of Torture (Charter)
rules.source: Charter of Fundamental Rights of the EU, Article 4 (mirrors ECHR Art. 3)
rules.summary: EU institutional restatement of the ECHR Article 3 prohibition. Binds EU bodies and Member States when implementing EU law.
rules.subject: officer

rules.title: Right to Liberty and Security (Charter)
rules.source: Charter of Fundamental Rights of the EU, Article 6
rules.summary: Mirrors ECHR Article 5: no arbitrary arrest, lawful procedure required, prompt information of reasons, prompt judicial review.
rules.subject: officer

rules.title: UN Convention Against Torture — Definition
rules.source: UN Convention Against Torture (1984), Article 1
rules.summary: Defines torture as severe pain or suffering, physical or mental, intentionally inflicted by or with consent of public official, for purpose of obtaining information, punishment, intimidation, or discrimination.
rules.subject: officer

rules.title: Proportionality of Police Use of Force
rules.source: ECHR Articles 2 and 3; UN Basic Principles on the Use of Force and Firearms (1990); Council of Europe Recommendation Rec(2001)10
rules.summary: Force used must be strictly proportionate to threat. De-escalation required where feasible. Firearms only when strictly unavoidable for protection of life.
rules.subject: officer

rules.title: Officer Identification Requirement
rules.source: Council of Europe European Code of Police Ethics (2001), Article 45; ECHR Article 3 procedural limb (identification enables accountability)
rules.summary: Police officers performing duties must be identifiable — by name, badge, or service number — to enable accountability. Removal or concealment of identification during force is a procedural failing.
rules.subject: officer

rules.title: Duty of De-escalation
rules.source: UN Basic Principles on the Use of Force (1990), Principles 4–5; Council of Europe Code of Police Ethics, Articles 35–37
rules.summary: Law enforcement officials shall, as far as possible, apply non-violent means before resorting to force. Communication, distance, time, and negotiation are required where feasible.
rules.subject: officer

rules.title: Criminal Threats Against Public Official
rules.source: Most EU Member State penal codes (e.g., Italian Codice Penale Art. 612; German StGB §§ 113, 241; French Code Pénal Art. 433-3)
rules.summary: Serious threats of violence against police officers performing official duties are criminal offences across EU jurisdictions, with aggravation when officer is on duty.
rules.subject: citizen

rules.title: Assault on a Public Official
rules.source: Italian Codice Penale Art. 336–337 (resistenza/violenza a pubblico ufficiale); German StGB § 113; French Code Pénal Art. 433-6
rules.summary: Physical violence against a police officer on duty is criminalised across EU jurisdictions. Includes spitting, striking, and any physical interference with arrest.
rules.subject: citizen

rules.title: Resistance to Public Official
rules.source: Italian Codice Penale Art. 337; analogous statutes across EU Member States
rules.summary: Active physical resistance — distinguished from passive non-compliance or verbal protest — to a public official performing duties is a criminal offence. Verbal protest alone is protected expression.
rules.subject: citizen

rules.title: Flight from Lawful Stop
rules.source: Member State traffic and criminal codes (e.g., Italian Codice della Strada Art. 192; German StGB § 142 analogues)
rules.summary: Failure to stop upon lawful police signal, particularly in a motor vehicle, is a criminal offence aggravated when reckless.
rules.subject: citizen

rules.title: Obstruction of Investigation
rules.source: Member State penal codes (e.g., Italian Codice Penale Art. 378 favoreggiamento; German StGB § 258)
rules.summary: Destroying evidence, lying to investigators about identity, or aiding a suspect's escape constitutes obstruction across EU jurisdictions.
rules.subject: citizen
